title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who talk about their pets and what those animals mean to them. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: More Than a Pet: Four People on Their Animal Companions

texts.A:
### Rachel, 34, Nurse
I've had my cat, Miso, for six years, and she has been the single best thing for my mental health. [43]After long, exhausting shifts at the hospital, just having her curl up on my lap the moment I sit down on the sofa makes everything feel more manageable.[/43] I know it sounds clichéd, but the research really does support what I feel instinctively. [47]What I find remarkable about Miso is how she seems to sense when I'm particularly stressed – she stays closer than usual on those days, as if she is keeping watch.[/47] People who haven't owned a cat often don't understand this kind of quiet companionship. [51]One thing I never expected was how much Miso would connect me with my neighbours – she wanders next door regularly, and now I have a far warmer relationship with the couple there than I ever did before.[/51]
texts.B:
### Marcus, 48, Accountant
[44]I adopted Bruno from a rescue centre after a particularly difficult divorce – I needed company and he needed a home, so it felt like the right thing to do.[/44] He's a large, clumsy Labrador who has caused a fair share of chaos in my flat, but I wouldn't change a thing. [48]The discipline of walking him twice a day has completely transformed my routine – I now spend far more time outdoors than I ever did before owning him, and I've lost nearly eight kilograms, which my doctor is delighted about.[/48] [52]The main challenge has been the cost – vet bills, food, insurance and boarding when I travel for work add up to a surprisingly large sum each month, so I now budget for him the way I budget for any essential expense.[/52]
texts.C:
### Priya, 27, PhD Student
I live in a tiny studio flat and knew I couldn't have a dog or cat, so I got a rabbit instead. [45]People tend to assume rabbits are uninteresting pets, but Pepper is genuinely one of the most curious and entertaining animals I've ever encountered.[/45] She races around the flat in the evenings in what I can only describe as pure joy, and watching her never fails to make me laugh. [49]During some of the most stressful periods of writing my PhD thesis, spending even ten minutes just watching Pepper was enough to reset my concentration and calm my nerves.[/49] I've also become part of an online community of rabbit owners and have learned a huge amount about rabbit behaviour, diet and welfare that I simply wasn't aware of before.
texts.D:
### Owen, 62, Retired Teacher
I took up fishkeeping at the age of sixty as a way of filling time constructively after I retired. [46]Setting up and maintaining an aquarium requires far more knowledge and attention than most people realise – water chemistry, feeding schedules and the compatibility of different species all have to be carefully considered.[/46] I find the process genuinely intellectually stimulating. [50]I spend at least an hour each morning simply observing the fish and watching their interactions, which has made me surprisingly patient – something my wife says I never used to be.[/50] I've since joined a local fishkeeping club whose members are among the most knowledgeable and enthusiastic hobbyists I've ever met.

questions.question: mentions their pet having a calming effect on them after a tiring day at work?
questions.explanation: Rachel says that after long, exhausting shifts at the hospital, having her cat curl up on her lap makes everything feel more manageable.

questions.question: acquired their pet during a difficult period in their personal life?
questions.explanation: Marcus adopted Bruno after a difficult divorce, saying he needed company and the dog needed a home.

questions.question: challenges a widespread assumption people make about their type of pet?
questions.explanation: Priya says people tend to assume rabbits are uninteresting, but describes hers as genuinely curious and entertaining.

questions.question: describes the unexpected complexity involved in caring for their pet?
questions.explanation: Owen explains that setting up an aquarium requires knowledge of water chemistry, feeding schedules and species compatibility.

questions.question: describes their pet appearing to detect changes in their emotional state?
questions.explanation: Rachel says Miso seems to sense when she is particularly stressed and stays closer than usual on those days.

questions.question: says owning a pet has made them considerably more physically active?
questions.explanation: Marcus says walking Bruno twice a day has transformed his routine – he now spends far more time outdoors and has lost nearly eight kilograms.

questions.question: describes their pet as a way of managing pressure during a demanding project?
questions.explanation: Priya says that spending ten minutes watching Pepper was enough to reset her concentration and calm her nerves during her PhD thesis.

questions.question: credits their pet with bringing about a change in their own character?
questions.explanation: Owen says that observing his fish has made him surprisingly patient, something his wife confirms he never used to be.

questions.question: describes an unexpected benefit for their social relationships as a result of owning their pet?
questions.explanation: Rachel says Miso wandering next door has led to a far warmer relationship with her neighbours than she had before.

questions.question: refers to the considerable financial demands of pet ownership as something they have had to plan for?
questions.explanation: Marcus mentions that vet bills, food, insurance and boarding add up to a large sum and that he now budgets for it like any essential expense.
